name: GPU transcription throughput without quality loss

description: Baseline GPU throughput benchmark and harness setup. Added autoresearch.md, autoresearch.sh, a dedicated CUDA benchmark binary, and deterministic transcript references for short and repeated workloads. Current baseline is 924.299ms for the 60-second repeated fixture with short_exact=1, long_exact=1, and short_keyword_hits=6.

Tried increasing the transcription chunk size from 30s to 120s to reduce repeated prompt and chunk overhead on the GPU. It improved wall_ms substantially, but long_exact dropped from 1 to 0 because the 60-second repeated workload lost one repeated segment in the transcript, so this is a real quality regression and must be rejected.

Tried eliminating tiny tail chunks by folding a sub-1s remainder back into earlier chunks, which reduced the 60-second synthetic workload from three chunks to two and improved wall_ms. The speedup was not acceptable because long_exact fell to 0: shifting the chunk boundary changed the repeated transcript ('tsunzhu' -> 'tsunzuo' and other formatting differences), so this chunk-boundary optimization is not quality preserving.

Tried replacing CUDA flash-attn with a manual matmul/softmax attention path for single-token decode steps, hoping to cut kernel-launch overhead during generation. It was much slower in practice and also changed the long repeated transcript (long_exact=0), so the CUDA decode path should stay on flash-attn.

Optimized mel extraction by precomputing each mel filter's non-zero FFT-bin range and skipping zero-weight bins during filterbank accumulation. This preserves the exact short and long transcripts while improving the primary GPU benchmark from 924.299ms to 861.699ms (~6.8% faster, 69.65x realtime).

Tried precomputing FFT bit-reversal indices to remove the branchy per-frame permutation math in the custom mel FFT. Quality stayed exact, but the result (889.511ms) was still slower than the current kept mel-range optimization at 861.699ms, so this extra complexity is not worth keeping.

Optimized the sparse mel filterbank path further by replacing the iterator-heavy zip/map/sum accumulation with a tight indexed loop over the active FFT-bin range for each mel band. This kept short_exact=1 and long_exact=1 while improving the GPU benchmark from 861.699ms to 830.156ms (~3.7% faster on top of the previous mel-range win, ~10.2% faster than the original baseline).

Tried removing per-sample bounds checks from the Hann-window application by assuming every frame is a full `n_fft` window. That assumption was wrong for very short trailing chunks (<400 samples): the benchmark hit a panic on the tiny final chunk, so this version is invalid.

Tried a fast path for full Hann windows to avoid per-sample bounds checks in the common case, while keeping the safe partial-window fallback for tiny tail chunks. Quality stayed exact, but the result (874.506ms) was still slower than the current kept implementation at 830.156ms, so this change does not help enough to keep.

Tried removing bounds checks from the hot sparse mel accumulation loop with an unsafe pointer walk over the active FFT-bin slice for each mel band. Quality stayed exact, but it still regressed versus the current kept implementation (856.263ms vs 830.156ms), so the extra unsafe complexity is not justified.

Tried a release-profile tuning pass with thin LTO and a single codegen unit to see whether better host-side code generation would speed the CPU mel path. Runtime quality stayed exact, but the benchmark still regressed versus the current best (877.978ms vs 830.156ms) and rebuilds became much slower, so this profile change is not worth keeping.

Tried shaving generation overhead by replacing argmax-to-vec extraction with `to_scalar::<u32>()` and using direct `(1,1)` token tensors. The scalar extraction change was wrong because Candle's argmax result here is rank-1, so this version crashed at runtime.

Tried a small generation-loop cleanup: use shaped `Tensor::from_slice` for prompt/token tensors and extract argmax results via `i(0)?.to_scalar::<u32>()` instead of materializing a one-element vec. The transcript stayed exact, but runtime regressed versus the current best (871.651ms vs 830.156ms), so this micro-optimization is not worth keeping.

Tried parallelizing mel-frame computation with Rayon when the spectrogram is large enough, using per-worker FFT scratch buffers. The output stayed exact and it nearly matched the current best, but it still came in slower (838.160ms vs 830.156ms), so the extra dependency and parallel complexity are not justified for this workload.

Tried manually unrolling the sparse mel dot product four lanes at a time to coax better code generation in the hot filterbank loop. Quality stayed exact, but it regressed badly versus the current best (862.419ms vs 830.156ms), so the compiler's original loop generation is better here.

Tried forcing more inlining in the custom FFT hot path (`magnitude_spectrum_with_buffers` and `fft_inplace`) to reduce call overhead in mel extraction. Quality stayed exact, but the benchmark regressed versus the current best (864.093ms vs 830.156ms), so these inline hints are not helping.

Tried explicitly guarding the per-token debug decode log behind `tracing::enabled!(DEBUG)` so token-string decoding would never happen unless debug logging is on. The transcript stayed exact, but runtime still regressed versus the current best (858.688ms vs 830.156ms), which suggests tracing was not the bottleneck.

Tried disabling the causal flag for single-token CUDA decode steps under the assumption that the KV cache already contains only past tokens. In practice it was slower and changed the long repeated transcript (`tsunzhu` -> `tsunzuo` in later chunks), so flash-attn's causal path is still required for correctness here.

Tried switching CUDA inference from BF16 to F16 to see whether this GPU prefers FP16 tensor-core paths. Quality stayed exact on both transcript guards, but throughput still regressed versus the current BF16 implementation (855.395ms vs 830.156ms), so BF16 remains the better choice here.

Tried caching the static ChatML prompt work inside `Qwen3ASRModel`: precompute the pre-audio and post-audio token sequences and their embeddings once at model load instead of rebuilding them for every chunk. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 885.224ms, so prompt tokenization/embedding setup was not a meaningful bottleneck in this benchmark.

Tried processing the static prompt prefix, audio embeddings, and prompt suffix as separate decoder passes so the KV cache could carry state across segments instead of building one concatenated prompt tensor. The transcript stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 930.979ms, so this segmented-prefix approach adds overhead instead of helping.

name: GPU transcription throughput without quality loss (correct WAV workload)

description: Rebased the autoresearch session onto a corrected benchmark workload. Fixed the WAV fixture loader in the benchmark and integration tests to parse RIFF chunks properly instead of assuming a fixed 44-byte header, updated the repeated-workload reference transcript, and re-initialized the session on the true 60.0-second audio workload. New baseline on the corrected CUDA benchmark is 793.356ms with short_exact=1 and long_exact=1.

Retried larger transcription chunks on the corrected 60.0-second workload by increasing `Qwen3ASRModel::CHUNK_SAMPLES` to 60 seconds so the repeated benchmark would run as a single chunk. It improved wall_ms substantially, but long_exact still dropped to 0 because one repeated segment disappeared, so even on the corrected benchmark this chunk-size increase is a real quality regression.

Noise-check rerun of the corrected-workload baseline with no code changes. The exact-quality transcript still passed, and wall_ms came in slightly lower at 788.342ms, which helps establish the new session's measurement noise but does not represent a real code improvement.

Tried squeezing decoder outputs down to 2D `(batch, vocab)` logits so the generation loop could skip the redundant per-step slice that extracts the last token from a `(batch, 1, vocab)` tensor. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 824.478ms, so the extra squeeze/view work is worse than leaving the existing shape alone.

Tried specializing the generation loop for the default greedy/no-repetition-penalty case so it could bypass `sample_token`'s extra branching during every decode step. The transcript stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 816.691ms, so the duplicated greedy path does not help on the corrected workload.

Optimized the decoder's single-token hot path by skipping the redundant `narrow(1, seq_len - 1, 1)` in `Qwen3Decoder::forward_embeds` when `seq_len == 1`. This path runs on every autoregressive decode step, preserved short_exact=1 and long_exact=1 across repeated confirmation runs, and improved the corrected-workload benchmark from 793.356ms to 782.628ms (~1.4% faster).

Noise-check rerun of the current `seq_len == 1` decoder fast path. Quality stayed exact, but this confirmation run landed slower than baseline (813.976ms), which reinforces that the measured ~1.4% win is within current benchmark noise rather than clearly established.

Tried isolating the token-tensor creation micro-optimization by replacing `Tensor::new(...).unsqueeze(0)` with a directly shaped `Tensor::from_slice(..., (1, 1), ...)` for each decode step, while keeping the current decoder hot-path win in place. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 806.637ms, so the shaped constructor is not helping here.

Tried a `seq_len == 1` rotary-embedding fast path that indexes a single precomputed sin/cos row instead of using `narrow(..., len=1)` during decode. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 809.680ms, so this extra branch does not help the decoder hot path.

Tried a small decode-loop housekeeping bundle: reserve capacity for generated tokens, cache the common single-stop-token case, and pre-check whether DEBUG tracing is enabled before decoding token strings for logs. Quality stayed exact, but runtime still regressed slightly to 799.344ms, so these per-token housekeeping tweaks are not worthwhile.

Tried hoisting the decoder attention softmax scale into a stored field so the hot path would stop recomputing `(head_dim as f32).powf(-0.5)` on every attention call. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 814.627ms, so this arithmetic hoist does not matter in practice.

Tried adding a `Qwen3Decoder::forward_token_id` hot path that indexes the embedding table directly for each generated token instead of building a one-token input tensor. The first attempt failed to compile because the new helper used tensor indexing without importing `IndexOp`.

Retried the `Qwen3Decoder::forward_token_id` hot path with the compile fix in place. It indexed the embedding table directly for each generated token instead of building a one-token input tensor. Quality stayed exact, but runtime still regressed to 799.965ms, so this decode-path specialization is not worthwhile.

Tried hoisting the audio encoder's flash-attention sequence-length tensor and max-window calculation out of each encoder layer so CUDA varlen attention could reuse them across the whole forward pass. The first attempt failed to compile because `Tensor::new` was given `&Vec<u32>` instead of a supported ndarray input.

Retried the audio-encoder flash-attention hoist with a compile fix: precompute the CUDA varlen sequence-length tensor and max window once per encoder forward pass instead of rebuilding them inside every attention layer. Quality stayed exact, but runtime still regressed slightly to 799.231ms, so the per-layer seqlens setup was not a meaningful bottleneck.

Tried increasing the audio encoder's inference attention window (`n_window_infer`) from 800 to 3000 so each 30-second chunk would use a single larger local-attention window instead of several smaller ones. Runtime was roughly unchanged, but long_exact dropped to 0 because the transcript changed from the established repeated-workload wording (`tsunzhu`) to a different normalization (`sun zhu`), so this inference-window change is not quality preserving under the current benchmark guardrail.

Tried adding a targeted `prefill_embeds` path in `Qwen3Decoder` so the static prompt prefix and audio embeddings could advance the KV cache without paying for norm/lm-head logits that are immediately discarded. It produced a sizable speedup, but long_exact dropped to 0 because the repeated transcript shifted from the established wording (`tsunzhu`) to `sun zhu`, so this KV-prefill refactor is not quality preserving as implemented.

Tried a lighter KV-prefill refactor: advance the cache only for the short static prefix with a new `prefill_embeds` path, then run the larger audio+assistant suffix in one normal decoder pass. It was even faster, but long_exact still dropped to 0 with the same wording shift (`tsunzhu` -> `sun zhu`), so even prefix-only KV prefill changes output and is not acceptable under the current quality guard.

Tried downgrading several hot-path runtime shape validations in `Qwen3ASRModel` to `debug_assert!` checks so release inference would stop paying those guards on every chunk. Quality stayed exact, but runtime still regressed slightly to 797.524ms, so these validation branches were not a meaningful cost.

Tried hard-coding the last-logit slice in the generation loop to `i((.., 0, ..))` instead of re-reading the sequence dimension each step, based on the decoder always returning a single-token logit slice during generation. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 802.845ms, so the more explicit fixed index does not help.

Tried routing the decoder's single-token CUDA decode steps through `flash_attn_varlen` instead of the regular flash-attn entrypoint, to see whether the varlen kernel handles the `(1 query, growing KV cache)` case more efficiently. The first attempt failed to compile because Candle's `Tensor::new` needed borrowed slice inputs for the tiny sequence-length tensors.

Retried the decoder's single-token CUDA path with `flash_attn_varlen` instead of the regular flash-attn entrypoint, using tiny `[0,1]` / `[0,cache_len]` seqlens tensors for the one-query decode case. Quality stayed exact, but runtime regressed to 820.112ms, so the standard flash-attn kernel remains the better choice here.

Noise-check rerun of the current kept decoder single-token hot path. Quality stayed exact, but the result landed back near baseline at 798.747ms, which confirms the current ~1.4% improvement is still not clearly separated from benchmark noise.

Noise-check rerun of the current CUDA baseline at the kept decoder single-token fast path. Quality stayed exact on both transcript guards and this run came in slightly faster than the recorded best (790.645ms vs 793.356ms), but there was no code change, so this is just benchmark noise rather than a real new optimization.

Pipelined CPU mel extraction across chunk boundaries for multi-chunk GPU transcriptions. `Qwen3ASRModel::transcribe` now computes the next chunk's mel spectrogram on a background thread while the current chunk is running through the CUDA encoder/decoder, then reuses the precomputed mel data in a new `transcribe_chunk_from_mel` path. This preserves short_exact=1 and long_exact=1 while improving wall_ms from 793.356ms to 772.933ms (~2.6% faster, 77.63x realtime).

Tried replacing the generation loop's one-element `to_vec1::<u32>()?[0]` argmax extraction with a scalar path (`argmax(...).i(0)?.to_scalar::<u32>()`) to shave per-token CPU bookkeeping. Quality stayed exact, but wall_ms was effectively unchanged and fractionally worse than the current best (772.967ms vs 772.933ms), so this micro-optimization is not worth keeping.

Tried constructing the mel tensor directly in BF16 on CUDA by converting the CPU mel buffer to `half::bf16` first, so the hot path could skip Candle's `to_dtype(BF16)` cast after upload. Quality stayed exact, but wall_ms regressed to 774.588ms, so the extra CPU-side conversion costs more than the saved GPU cast.

Cached the static ChatML prompt token IDs inside `Qwen3ASRModel` at load time, so each chunk reuses the same precomputed `system/user/assistant` token vectors instead of re-tokenizing and rebuilding them during generation. Quality stayed exact on both transcript guards and wall_ms improved slightly from 772.933ms to 772.765ms, though this gain is close to the current noise floor.

Noise-check rerun of the current kept baseline after caching the static prompt token IDs. Quality stayed exact and this rerun came in slightly faster again at 772.199ms, which is directionally consistent with the kept change but still not a separate code improvement.
